topic: Flagship new topic: support-fragmented causal transport across multiple source trials. Characterize sharp identification of a target-population ATE when no single source trial satisfies positivity on the target covariate support, but the collection of source trials jointly covers the target. The proposed kernel is a finite-support Hall-type if-and-only-if certificate: target ATE is identified exactly when every target support atom can be assigned to a source trial with experimental treatment support and invariant conditional potential-outcome law; below the certificate give a sharp partial-ID interval and explicit two-source witness. This should be positioned against Pearl-Bareinboim multi-transportability and Dahabreh-style multiple-trial transport as a finite positivity/support-fragmentation frontier, not an upgrade of any banked topic.

open_problems.open_problem: Give a finite-support if-and-only-if certificate for exact target-population ATE identification under support-fragmented multi-trial transport.

source_refs.claim: Develops causally interpretable meta-analysis from multiple randomized trials to a target population, including identification and efficient doubly robust estimation under multi-trial transport assumptions.
source_refs.author: Dahabreh, Robertson, Petito, Hernan, Steingrimsson

source_refs.claim: States identifiability conditions for transporting inferences from one trial at a time and from a collection of randomized trials to a target population.
source_refs.author: Dahabreh, Petito, Robertson, Hernan, Steingrimsson

source_refs.claim: Generalizes graphical transportability to multiple heterogeneous domains but frames feasibility in selection-diagram and do-calculus terms rather than finite positivity atoms.
source_refs.author: Bareinboim, Lee, Honavar, Pearl

source_refs.excerpt: Cover succeeds iff P^*_x(Y) is identified from the k selected source laws and constraints.
Sharp diagnostic distinguishing add another source can identify the effect from source margins can still hide two target effects.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The multi-trial transport papers give sufficient identification formulas and estimators under pooled participation and treatment positivity, while the Pearl-Bareinboim line gives graphical completeness for a broader do-calculus problem. The unresolved frontier is a finite-support, potential-outcome/econometric certificate that isolates atom-level source assignment, treatment support, and conditional potential-outcome invariance as necessary and sufficient for point identification; the prior CausalSmith source-cover proposal also flagged this converse/completeness direction but did not close the nonidentification witness.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem proving equivalence between target ATE point identification and existence of an admissible cover of every target covariate atom by at least one source-trial-treatment cell, with a constructive formula and countermodels for failed cover conditions.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp partial-identification bounds for the target ATE when the multi-trial support-fragmentation certificate fails.

source_refs.claim: Reports partial- and point-identification results for ATR and ATE when randomized trial data have selective participation and external validity is not guaranteed.
source_refs.author: Kline, Tamer

source_refs.claim: Shows transportability positivity violations leave nonpositive regions nonparametrically unidentified without additional synthesis or model assumptions.
source_refs.author: Zivich, Edwards, Shook-Sa, Lofgren, Lessler, Cole
source_refs.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series A

source_refs.claim: Operationalizes single-study transportability with inverse odds of sampling weights under sampling positivity and exchangeability.
source_refs.author: Westreich, Edwards, Lesko, Stuart, Cole

source_refs.excerpt: Sharp partial identification of CATT under bounded overlap violation via a critical overlap exponent.

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing partial-ID work handles selective trial participation broadly, and recent transportability-without-positivity work uses target restriction, covariate restriction, extrapolation, or statistical-simulation synthesis. What is missing is a sharp atomwise interval tied directly to a failed multi-source support certificate, preserving identified atom contributions and bounding only uncovered treatment-potential-outcome cells; the prior overlap-exponent bank entry signals related partial-ID interest but not the multi-trial finite-support law.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A closed-form sharp interval for bounded outcomes equal to the sum of identified target-atom contributions plus worst-case contributions for uncovered source-treatment cells, with attainable counterfactual laws proving both endpoints.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct minimal two-source witnesses separating single-trial positivity failure, pooled-source positivity success, and nonidentification after an atom-level invariance failure.

source_refs.claim: Analyzes extrapolating experimental program effects across sites and stresses overlap and population differences as central to credible external validity.
source_refs.author: Hotz, Imbens, Mortimer

source_refs.claim: Illustrates model-based standardization of randomized trial results to a specified target population and highlights effect-modifier measurement and modeling assumptions.
source_refs.author: Cole, Stuart

source_refs.claim: Motivates multi-trial transport by using a collection of trials to estimate target-population potential outcome means and ATEs.
source_refs.author: Dahabreh, Robertson, Petito, Hernan, Steingrimsson

open_problems.why_unsolved: The literature contains applications, examples, and simulations, but it does not provide a canonical finite two-source construction that cleanly distinguishes three regimes: each individual trial misses target support, the union of trials identifies by atomwise coverage, and the same observed laws become nonidentifying when one source-specific invariance clause is removed.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A two-binary-atom construction with source 1 covering one target atom and source 2 covering the other, followed by paired observationally equivalent laws that agree on all source and target covariate data but disagree on the target ATE after an admissibility clause fails.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize finite-support diagnostics and uncertainty for support-fragmented transport when atom membership, trial treatment support, and admissible source assignments are empirical rather than known.

source_refs.claim: Summarizes methods for extending causal inferences from one or more randomized trials and lists unidentifiable overlap among remaining limitations.
source_refs.author: Dahabreh, Stuart

source_refs.claim: Reviews generalizability and transportability methods, assumptions, heterogeneity tests, and differences between study and target populations.
source_refs.author: Degtiar, Rose
source_refs.venue: Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application

source_refs.claim: Derives semiparametric efficiency and doubly robust estimators for target ATE from multiple source trials under effect-measure transportability.
source_refs.author: Su, Rytgaard, Ravn, Eriksson

open_problems.why_unsolved: Current work emphasizes identification formulas, efficiency, software, or broad reviews, usually taking the relevant covariate support and positivity regime as a population-level assumption. Sparse finite support creates a distinct problem: the support certificate itself is estimated, near-empty source-treatment cells can change admissibility, and multiple source assignments can yield the same estimand but different finite-sample behavior.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A diagnostic-and-inference theorem or procedure that reports uncovered target mass, admissible source sets per atom, assignment sensitivity, and valid uncertainty statements for finite-support certificates estimated from sparse multi-trial data.

literature_map:
Cole and Stuart (2010, AJE) is an early standardization template for transporting one randomized trial to a target population.
Westreich, Edwards, Lesko, Stuart, and Cole (2017, AJE) operationalize transportability through inverse odds of sampling weights, with sampling positivity as a core condition.
Dahabreh, Petito, Robertson, Hernan, and Steingrimsson (2020, Epidemiology) formalize causally interpretable meta-analysis from multiple trials to one target population.
Dahabreh, Robertson, Petito, Hernan, and Steingrimsson (2023, Biometrics) give efficient doubly robust multi-trial estimators and pooled multi-trial identification conditions.
A related systematically-missing-covariate literature notes that participation positivity can be on the pooled set of trials, enabling broader target support than any individual trial.
Hotz, Imbens, and Mortimer (2005, Journal of Econometrics) is a key econometric ancestor: extrapolation across experimental sites depends on covariate overlap and program comparability.
Kline and Tamer (2018, Econometrics Journal) give partial identification for external validity and selective trial participation, but not a multi-source support-fragmentation geometry.
Zivich et al. (2025, JRSS-A) directly confront transportability positivity failure and emphasize that nonpositive regions are not nonparametrically identified without extra synthesis assumptions.
Degtiar and Rose (2023, Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application) synthesize generalizability/transportability assumptions, diagnostics, and heterogeneity concerns.
Su, Rytgaard, Ravn, and Eriksson (2024, arXiv) move toward multi-source efficiency under effect-measure transportability rather than full conditional potential-outcome transport.
Bareinboim, Lee, Honavar, and Pearl (2013, NeurIPS) provide graphical multi-environment transportability conditions, useful as contrast rather than the target econometric kernel.
The central tension is point identification by target standardization under pooled positivity versus sharp nonidentification outside the source support.
The live gap is a finite-support, atom-level, sharp iff certificate plus matching bounds below positivity, stated in potential-outcome/econometric terms rather than do-calculus axiomatics.
prior_proposal_map:
Inspected formalization bank proposals, reviews, gaps, states, and READMEs under doc/formalization only; no web was used by Track A.
Strongest direct signal is eid_data_fusion_k_source_v1: k partially overlapping source laws, source positivity, transport/generalizability positioning, and overlap-hedge completeness.
That prior is broader and observational-only; it is adjacent but not an upgrade of the new multi-source-trial support-fragmentation topic.
No prior proposal directly states a Dahabreh-style multiple-source-trial positivity-fragmentation/Hall atom assignment theorem.
pid_critical_overlap_exponent_v1 is relevant for positivity failure and sharp partial-ID intervals, but its prior run failed due to pipeline issues.
eid_proximal_completeness_v1 gives adjacent external-validity/two-sample support and transport-stability structure.
pid_ot_dte_marginal_v1 and pid_network_random_set_v1 provide finite-support support-function/witness patterns useful below a failed certificate.
The study-pipeline gaps supplied with this run were mostly LATE, Manski bounds, or interval-regression items and were not weighted above the topic-specific transport evidence.
